summary: Jeff Atwood argues that JavaScript, despite being initially dismissed by many programmers including himself, has become the indispensable programming language of the web. He traces JavaScript's journey from a 'toy language' to a mature platform, crediting the View Source culture and increasing browser capabilities for its success. While acknowledging JavaScript's frustrations—cross-browser issues, debugging pain, and imperfect design—he contends that its ubiquity and the viral nature of web development make it impossible to ignore. He pushes back against developers who avoid JavaScript by fleeing to alternatives like Silverlight, Flex, or Flash, arguing that understanding JavaScript's limitations is essential before seeking to transcend them.
key_points: JavaScript 'won' the web not because it was the best language, but because it was already everywhere when developers needed client-side interactivity
The View Source culture and copy-paste mashup capability gave the web its distributed evolution and made JavaScript's success possible
JavaScript is actually closer to functional languages like Lisp or Scheme than to C or Java, with first-class functions and closures
Despite alternatives from Microsoft, Adobe, and Sun, JavaScript's ubiquity guarantees its continued dominance
Developers should immerse themselves in JavaScript's limitations rather than blindly fighting them or fleeing to alternatives
money_quotes: If you're the last man left on earth, it doesn't matter how ugly you are when the women come to re-populate the planet.
JavaScript is the world's most ubiquitous computing runtime.
The best way to transcend the limitations of JavaScript is to immerse yourself in those limitations.
JavaScript is the lingua franca of the web. Ignore it at your peril.
You get lambdas without having to balance all those parens.
If you give up the viral soft collaboration of View Source and copy-and-paste mashups... you lose what made the web great, and what made the web win.
key_insight: JavaScript's dominance stems not from being the best language but from being universally available, and developers should embrace its limitations rather than flee to proprietary alternatives.
